title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
This project focused on a real smart-city problem in Gorey, Co. Wexford: how to move from reactive monitoring to proactive forecasting. The work combined IoT sensor data and external weather information to build a framework for predicting traffic congestion and PM2.5 pollution risk. The broader aim was to support better planning, earlier interventions, and more informed decision-making for public stakeholders.
What made the project especially interesting was that it linked two connected urban systems: traffic and air quality. Instead of treating them as isolated prediction tasks, the project used a two-stage framework that reflected the real relationship between vehicle activity, meteorological conditions, and pollutant concentration.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
This project focused on a real smart-city problem in Gorey, Co. Wexford: how to move from reactive monitoring to proactive forecasting. The work combined IoT sensor data and external weather information to build a framework for predicting traffic congestion and PM2.5 pollution risk. The broader aim was to support better planning, earlier interventions, and more informed decision-making for public stakeholders.
What made the project especially interesting was that it linked two connected urban systems: traffic and air quality. Instead of treating them as isolated prediction tasks, the project used a two-stage framework that reflected the real relationship between vehicle activity, meteorological conditions, and pollutant concentration.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
Problem / Context
Main Street in Gorey experiences congestion during peak hours, school drop-off periods, weekends, and tourist seasons. These traffic conditions contribute to air-pollution concerns, particularly around pollutants associated with road emissions. While sensor infrastructure already existed through the Box of Things deployment, the available data was largely descriptive rather than predictive.
That gap limited the practical value of the system. Without forecasting capability, local authorities are forced to respond after congestion or poor air quality has already occurred. The purpose of this project was to close that gap by designing a predictive framework that could support proactive traffic management and public-health awareness.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
Approach / Methodology
The project combined Box of Things traffic, air-quality, and environmental sensor data with external weather data from Open-Meteo and Met Éireann. Because the datasets came in different frequencies, an important part of the work involved preprocessing, time alignment, hourly resampling, and careful handling of missing values and outliers.
The modelling design had two stages. Stage 1 focused on one-hour-ahead traffic forecasting. Stage 2 used predicted traffic, weather variables, and lagged pollutant values to model PM2.5 concentration and exceedance risk. This structure was important because it better reflected how decision support would work in practice.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
What I did

Worked on a smart-city analytics framework combining IoT traffic, air-quality, and weather data.
Applied preprocessing, resampling, chronological ordering, and data-quality checks to build a usable modelling dataset.
Used feature engineering to capture time effects, lag structures, rolling statistics, and meteorological influence.
Compared modelling approaches including Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM, and baseline methods.
Followed a chronological validation strategy to avoid leakage and better simulate real forecasting conditions.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
Results / Outcomes
The project produced a structured forecasting framework rather than a standalone dashboard or deployed public system. Its main outcome was a reproducible modelling approach that connected traffic forecasting with PM2.5 prediction and exceedance-risk thinking.








Stage
Aim
Inputs




Stage 1
1-hour-ahead traffic forecasting
IoT traffic readings, time features, lagged values


Stage 2
PM2.5 prediction and exceedance-risk modelling
Predicted traffic, meteorology, lagged pollutant values

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
Why it matters
This project sits at the intersection of analytics, sustainability, and public-sector impact. Better traffic forecasting can support congestion planning, while better pollution forecasting can help identify higher-risk periods and support early warnings for vulnerable populations. The value of the work is not only in predictive accuracy, but in making sensor infrastructure more useful for actual decision-making.
It also showed how business analytics skills can transfer into civic and environmental settings. The same core ideas — forecasting, feature engineering, stakeholder communication, and dashboard-oriented outputs — are highly relevant outside commercial business problems.

title: Traffic & Air Pollution Prediction
text:
What I learned
The biggest lesson from this project was the importance of methodology in time-series work. Good forecasting is not just about training a model; it depends on leakage-safe validation, sensible time alignment, and building features that reflect how the system behaves over time.
I also learned that strong analytics projects need a communication layer. For a project like this, the final value depends on making technical results understandable for non-technical stakeholders such as councils, planners, and decision-makers.

title: Reflection
text:
Building this portfolio website has been one of the more unexpectedly rewarding experiences of my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University. Coming from a background with a growing interest in technology and data, I had some familiarity with analytical tools — but creating and publishing a personal website from scratch was an entirely new challenge.
The process began with getting to grips with Quarto — a publishing framework I had not worked with before. Understanding the relationship between .qmd source files, the _quarto.yml configuration, and the final rendered HTML output required patience and careful reading of documentation. Early on, small configuration errors caused the entire site to fail to render, which quickly taught me the value of incremental changes and version control.
One of the most valuable aspects of this project was learning to think about content structurally. Deciding what to include on each page — and how to organise the navbar, footer, and project listings — forced me to consider the audience: recruiters, academic supervisors, and future collaborators. This perspective directly influenced the writing across the site, pushing me toward a tone that is clear, professional, and purposeful.
Working with GitHub for deployment also deepened my understanding of version control workflows. Committing changes, pushing to a remote repository, and enabling GitHub Pages as the publishing source were skills that had previously felt abstract but now feel genuinely practical — and directly transferable to collaborative data projects in a professional setting.
The technical challenges — fixing broken file paths, correctly linking project pages, ensuring consistent rendering — closely mirrored real-world data pipeline problems: small upstream errors cascade into downstream failures, and systematic debugging is always more efficient than guesswork.
Overall, this project has strengthened my technical confidence and produced a professional artefact I am proud to share. It has also reinforced a broader lesson I am taking from my MSc: the ability to communicate analytical work clearly — to structure, present, and contextualise findings — is just as important as the analysis itself.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Sentiment analysis is one of the most practical NLP tasks in business because it helps organisations turn unstructured customer feedback into usable signals. In this project, I compared two different approaches to binary sentiment classification: a fine-tuned BERT model and a zero-shot generative AI workflow using Llama 3. The goal was not just to compare model scores, but to evaluate which approach made more sense for a realistic business use case.
I framed the project around a streaming-platform scenario where customer reviews could be used for two purposes: identifying strongly negative feedback for service escalation and supporting content curation decisions. This made the work more than a technical model comparison. It became an exercise in choosing the right AI approach under business constraints.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Sentiment analysis is one of the most practical NLP tasks in business because it helps organisations turn unstructured customer feedback into usable signals. In this project, I compared two different approaches to binary sentiment classification: a fine-tuned BERT model and a zero-shot generative AI workflow using Llama 3. The goal was not just to compare model scores, but to evaluate which approach made more sense for a realistic business use case.
I framed the project around a streaming-platform scenario where customer reviews could be used for two purposes: identifying strongly negative feedback for service escalation and supporting content curation decisions. This made the work more than a technical model comparison. It became an exercise in choosing the right AI approach under business constraints.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Problem / Context
Streaming platforms receive large volumes of user reviews, and manually reading them is not scalable. An automated sentiment pipeline can help surface dissatisfied customers earlier and give decision-makers a more systematic view of audience response. In practice, that could support customer service teams, recommendation workflows, and catalogue management.
The key question behind this project was simple: should a business use a traditional fine-tuned NLP model or a large generative model for this kind of classification task? That decision affects cost, speed, data requirements, explainability, and deployment complexity.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Approach / Methodology
To explore this, I used the Rotten Tomatoes dataset for binary sentiment classification. I fine-tuned bert-base-uncased using the Hugging Face Trainer pipeline and compared it against Llama 3 running locally through Ollama in a zero-shot setting.
The BERT workflow required labelled data, tokenisation, training, and evaluation. The Llama 3 workflow relied on prompt design and post-processing rather than task-specific training. I evaluated both models using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, while also considering practical trade-offs such as inference cost, scalability, flexibility, and privacy implications from local deployment.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
What I did

Built a fine-tuning pipeline for BERT using Hugging Face and bert-base-uncased.
Designed a zero-shot prompting workflow for Llama 3 running locally through Ollama.
Evaluated both models using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
Framed the comparison around a realistic streaming-platform decision-support use case.
Reflected on ethical AI issues such as transparency, fairness, privacy, and deployment governance.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Results / Outcomes



Model
Accuracy
Precision
Recall
F1




BERT
0.830
0.811
0.835
0.823


Llama 3
0.930
0.934
0.914
0.924



Llama 3 outperformed BERT in this comparison, but the result should be interpreted carefully. BERT was evaluated on 500 examples, while Llama 3 was evaluated on 200 examples due to runtime constraints. The outcome suggests that zero-shot generative AI can perform strongly on practical classification tasks, especially when labelled data is limited, but it does not automatically make it the better production choice.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Why it matters
This project matters because many organisations are now deciding whether to use task-specific models or general-purpose LLMs for applied NLP workflows. The answer is rarely just “use the model with the best score.” A business also needs to consider labelled data availability, cost, latency, reproducibility, governance, and how the model will be used by real teams.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
What I learned
This project changed the way I think about model selection. I went in expecting the fine-tuned classifier to win, but the generative model delivered stronger results in a low-data setting. That reinforced an important lesson: in applied analytics, the best model is the one that fits the business context, data availability, and operational constraints.
It also showed me that AI project evaluation should go beyond benchmark scores. Cost, reproducibility, interpretability, latency, and governance all matter when a model moves from notebook to business workflow.

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Tools / Skills

Python
Hugging Face Transformers
BERT fine-tuning
Llama 3 via Ollama
Prompt engineering
Model evaluation
NLP
Responsible AI

title: BERT vs Generative AI
text:
Takeaway
This project helped me connect technical NLP modelling with real business decision-making by comparing not just model performance, but also the practical trade-offs involved in deploying AI responsibly.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
The Fan Growth Engine was a strategy and analytics project focused on a major commercial question in sport: how do you turn one-off attendance into long-term fan relationships? The project examined the Football Association of Ireland’s fan development challenge and identified a structural retention problem hidden beneath strong public interest and major attendance moments.
Rather than treating fan growth as a simple marketing issue, the project approached it as a CRM and lifecycle design problem. The central argument was that the FAI already had interest, attention, and audience momentum, but lacked the data infrastructure and retention architecture required to convert those assets into sustained fan value.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
The Fan Growth Engine was a strategy and analytics project focused on a major commercial question in sport: how do you turn one-off attendance into long-term fan relationships? The project examined the Football Association of Ireland’s fan development challenge and identified a structural retention problem hidden beneath strong public interest and major attendance moments.
Rather than treating fan growth as a simple marketing issue, the project approached it as a CRM and lifecycle design problem. The central argument was that the FAI already had interest, attention, and audience momentum, but lacked the data infrastructure and retention architecture required to convert those assets into sustained fan value.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Problem / Context
The project identified three connected weaknesses. First, attendance was heavily parent-driven at younger ages, meaning support was often transactional rather than independently loyal. Second, there was a sharp drop-off around age 18, when young supporters disappeared from organisational data systems. Third, grassroots and ticketing data were siloed, preventing effective segmentation and personalised communication.
Taken together, these issues meant the organisation was repeatedly reacquiring fans it should have been retaining. That is commercially inefficient and strategically risky, especially during a period when women’s football in Ireland had strong momentum and visibility.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Approach / Methodology
The project used a sequence of frameworks to move from diagnosis to strategic design. These included PESTEL, SWOT, TOWS, Value Proposition Canvas, Customer Journey analysis, and Business Model Canvas. Rather than using frameworks as isolated academic labels, the project linked them together so that each one led to a clear strategic decision.
The final strategy centred on lifecycle segmentation across four groups: Kids, Teens, Students, and Adults. The student segment was identified as the critical intervention point because it represented the highest churn risk and one of the strongest long-term value opportunities.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
What I did

Contributed to analysing fan-retention and CRM issues within the FAI context.
Worked with strategic frameworks including PESTEL, SWOT, TOWS, Customer Journey, and Business Model Canvas.
Helped shape a lifecycle segmentation strategy across Kids, Teens, Students, and Adults.
Explored the role of data architecture, CRM workflows, and retention triggers in fan development.
Connected strategic recommendations to commercial logic including CAC, LTV, and revenue growth thinking.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Results / Outcomes
Two standout initiatives helped make the strategy practical. The first was the Student ID Scheme and Lucky 8 Draw, which combined affordability, CRM data capture, and repeat-attendance incentives into one mechanism. The second was the Players Teach Programme, which linked community engagement with future conversion by turning grassroots contact into a consent-based fan pipeline.
What made these initiatives strong was that they were not random campaign ideas. They were designed to solve a specific structural problem: the loss of fan relationships during life-stage transition.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Why it matters
This project was important because it showed how business analytics can be applied beyond dashboards and predictive models. Here, the value came from diagnosing a systems problem, structuring evidence, segmenting users, and designing a commercially credible solution.
It also highlighted the power of CRM thinking. A supporter does not become loyal by accident; retention usually depends on timing, relevance, incentives, and the organisation’s ability to act on its own data.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
What I learned
This project strengthened my ability to connect analytics with strategy. I learned how segmentation, lifecycle logic, and commercial metrics such as customer acquisition cost and lifetime value can shape better decisions even when the output is not a machine-learning model.
It also taught me that data problems are often organisational problems in disguise. If systems are fragmented and relationships are not tracked properly, growth becomes harder no matter how much public interest already exists.

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Tools / Skills

CRM strategy
Customer lifecycle design
Segmentation
Business model thinking
SWOT / TOWS
Value Proposition Canvas
Customer Journey Mapping
Commercial analytics
Sports business strategy

title: The Fan Growth Engine
text:
Takeaway
This project helped me show that I can apply analytical thinking not only to technical modelling, but also to strategic growth problems where data, customer behaviour, and commercial design are closely connected.
